speaker: Guest
text: Undeclared wars are commonplace. Tragically, our government engages in preemptive war, otherwise known as aggression, with no complaints from the American people. Sadly,

speaker: Guest
text: we have become accustomed to living with the illegitimate use of force by government. To develop a truly free society, the issue of initiating force must be understood and rejected.

speaker: Guest
text: What if sometimes, to love your country, you had to alter or abolish the government? What if Jefferson was right? What if that government is best which governs least?

speaker: Guest
text: What if it is dangerous to be right when the government is wrong? What if it is better to perish fighting for freedom than to live as a slave? What if freedom's greatest hour of danger is now?

speaker: Host
text: Hi, everyone. Judge Andrew Napolitano here for Judging Freedom. Today is Tuesday, April 14th, 2026. Professor John Mearsheimer will be with us in just a moment on just how did Netanyahu and company trick the president.

speaker: Host
text: But first this.

speaker: Host
text: Don't you just cringe when people say, I told you so? Sorry. I told you gold and silver would reap the benefits due to excessive money printing, inflation, and global uncertainty. It's here. It's happened. Gold and silver reached all-time highs.

speaker: Host
text: Did you call Lear Capital and buy some? It's not too late. Experts are predicting higher prices ahead. Why? Nothing has changed. Geopolitical chaos, cost of living crises and a weaker dollar are driving central banks to boost their gold reserves.

speaker: Host
text: Forecast suggests gold could hit $6,000 an ounce and silver $200 an ounce. Even Morgan Stanley ditched the 60-40 rule for 60-20-20, putting 20% into precious metals. They're getting educated, and you should too. Call the best in the business.

speaker: Host
text: As in the people I trust, Lear Capital. Get their reports, get the facts, get some gold and silver. Tell them the judge sent you and get up to $20,000 in bonus gold or silver. Call 800-511-4620 or go to leargujudnap.com.

speaker: Host
text: Professor Mehrshammer, welcome here, my dear friend. Thank you for accommodating my schedule as you always so graciously do. Before we get into... Whether or not, and certainly whether,

speaker: Host
text: the Israelis tricked President Trump into believing this war would be over in 96 hours, I want to ask you a few questions about more recent events.

speaker: Host
text: Were the events in Islamabad this past weekend a true negotiation intended to produce a long-term peace?

speaker: Guest
text: It sure doesn't look like that. I mean, I'd make two points.

speaker: Guest
text: One is it appears that the Americans thought they were in the driver's seat almost completely and could dictate terms on the nuclear issue to the Iranians. And if you look at what we were demanding,

speaker: Guest
text: it's hard to believe that this was a serious negotiating position that we were. We were putting forward. The Iranians have long made it clear they're not going to completely give up their nuclear enrichment capability, period.

speaker: Guest
text: End of story. And the idea that we were going to force them to do it when we're not winning the war makes no sense. Second point I'd make, and this is, I think, a very important point.

speaker: Guest
text: To get any form of negotiated agreement that puts an end to this conflict in a meaningful way. You have to deal with a whole host of issues. The nuclear issue is just one of them. And I don't see any evidence that those other issues were addressed.

speaker: Guest
text: And this involves things like reparations, who controls the strait for the long term, what happens with Hezbollah and Israel, what happens with sanctions on Iran, what happens with American military. curry bases in the region.

speaker: Guest
text: These are all issues that either the Iranians or the Americans have put on the table. And I don't see any evidence that they were discussed. It looks like everything revolved around the nuclear issue.

speaker: Guest
text: And even there, they couldn't get out of the batter's box, but less to first base. So I'm not sure where this all leaves us.

speaker: Host
text: So you have the Speaker of the Parliament and the Foreign Minister of Iran negotiating with the He was the vice president of the country that had just dispatched a few hundred.

speaker: Host
text: their planes and a few hundred troops to try and steal the enriched uranium and of course that attempted theft failed when one of the planes crashed and they had to turn it into a rescue mission you also had presence of the negotiation two people that the Iranians said don't bring them

speaker: Host
text: Don't bring Litkov and Kushner because we don't trust them. They duped us twice, both in June and in February, leading us to believe negotiations were going on while you were planning an attack.

speaker: Host
text: Would Kushner and Kushner show up as the Israeli eyes and ears on Vance? What do you think?

speaker: Guest
text: Well, I think it's very important to understand that the reason you are.

speaker: Guest
text: highly unlikely to ever get any form of meaningful agreement and put this whole problem to bed is because the Israelis are in the driver's seat when it comes to how the Americans negotiate with the Iranians and the Israelis are firmly committed to at least achieving regime change in Tehran and getting a regime that will surrender.

speaker: Guest
text: Israel and the United States in place or What they'd really like to do is wreck Iran. So given Israel's agenda and given the tremendous leverage that Israel has over the United States, how do you ever solve this conflict?

speaker: Guest
text: The Israelis are not going to agree to leave Iran in a position where it can grow economically and it still maintains its enrichment. equipment capability. They simply won't accept that. They'll continue to cause trouble vis-a-vis Iran.

speaker: Guest
text: And because of the power of the lobby, we'll go along. And by the way, you saw Israel's influence, as you pointed out, in Islamabad. I mean, not only did you have two Israeli assets standing behind Vance and looking over his shoulder,

speaker: Guest
text: but it's quite clear that Vance actually called Netanyahu once during the negotiations, and he was in constant contact with Trump and with Rubio, both of whom sure were in contact with the Israelis. And on top of all that.

speaker: Guest
text: Vice President Vance wants to be the president of the United States. He wants to win the 2028 election, as does Marco Rubio. And that tells you that both Vance and Rubio will dance to Israel's tune. So the question is,

speaker: Guest
text: how do you ever put together a negotiating position, talking from an American perspective, where you can get some sort of meaningful agreement? with Iran and the answer is I don't think you can

speaker: Host
text: How can Vance possibly dig himself out of the hole that he's already in politically? I mean, he's there ostensibly to negotiate the ceasefire of a war that he opposed right up until the last minute. He had just finished three days of campaigning.

speaker: Host
text: English to Hungarian people for Viktor Orban who suffered a resounding overwhelming defeat and he talks to President Trump 11 times in 21 hours meaning he obviously had no authority whatsoever to agree on anything without running everything past Trump and then of course I'm going to use Prime Minister Netanyahu's word

speaker: Host
text: He reported to the Israeli Prime Minister Chris number three

speaker: Host
text: I spoke yesterday with Vice President J.D. Vance.

speaker: Host
text: He called me from his plane on the way back from Washington.

speaker: Host
text: He reported to me in detail, as the people of this administration do every day, on the development of the negotiations.

speaker: Host
text: In this case, the explosion in the negotiations-the explosion came from the American side, which was not willing to tolerate the blatant violation of the agreement to enter negotiations by Iran. Essentially,

speaker: Host
text: the agreement was that there would be a ceasefire and Iran would immediately open the crossings. They didn't do that. The Americans were not willing to accept it.

speaker: Host
text: He also conveyed to me that the central issue on the table from the perspective of President Trump and the United States is the removal of all enriched material and ensuring that there is no more enrichment in the coming years,

speaker: Host
text: and this could be for decades.

speaker: Host
text: Well, one key word and one fascinating phrase he reported to me in detail as the people of of this administration do every day. Report to him. Does this surprise you?

speaker: Guest
text: No, this is just more evidence for what I said a few minutes ago. The Israelis basically own us.

speaker: Guest
text: They're largely driving our negotiating position with the Iranians. And this is why I said to you, how do we ever resolve this conflict when the Israelis are committed to wrecking Iran and making sure Iran has no nuclear capability at all?

speaker: Guest
text: And the answer is there's no way you can resolve this in some meaningful way. This conflict is going to go on and on and on.

speaker: Guest
text: on because the Israelis wanted to go on and on and on and we are not in a position to stand up to the Israelis it's as simple as that

speaker: Host
text: So when the president is confronted with intelligence from his own intelligence agencies that basically say this is not going to be an easy war to win. And a foreign intelligence agency, so don't worry about it, it'll be over within 96 hours,

speaker: Host
text: we'll kill the Ayatollah, we'll foment riots in the streets, the rioters will take over the government. Have you ever heard of an American president rejecting the intel of his own agencies and accepting that of a foreign entity? I'm speaking,

speaker: Host
text: of course, of Mossad.

speaker: Guest
text: No, I think what happened in these meetings between Trump and Netanyahu and Trump and his advisors is really quite extraordinary. And it's extraordinary in the sense that all his principal advisors, you might want to use the word the deep state.

speaker: Guest
text: They all were flashing orange lights, if not red lights, and telling him that attacking Iran was not a good idea.

speaker: Host
text: Except for Pistol Pete.

speaker: Guest
text: Yeah, except for Pistol Pete, but he almost doesn't count because everybody understands he's really not a serious thinker. I'm sure that President Trump even realizes that at this point in time,

speaker: Guest
text: but except for him, all the other people, including John Ratcliffe, Ratcliffe, according

speaker: Host
text: Yes.

speaker: Guest
text: to reports,

speaker: Guest
text: and John Ratcliffe is super pro-Israel, but as the head of the CIA, he was undoubtedly talking to all sorts of his analysts who were telling him that this was a cockamamie idea. But it was the Israelis in the form of David Barnea, who was the head of...

speaker: Guest
text: who is the head of Mossad and Prime Minister Netanyahu, who basically bamboozled President Trump into thinking that he could win a quick and decisive victory. But again,

speaker: Guest
text: what's really remarkable about this is that his principal advisors were telling him this is a bad idea and that he was going to get himself into a well of a lot of trouble, which is exactly what has happened.

speaker: Host
text: Well, the Israelis got him into the war. Are they going to get him out? No.

speaker: Guest
text: No, they're going to keep them in. That's

speaker: Host
text: Right.

speaker: Guest
text: my point to you. You have to understand that, you know, everybody's talking about how we're going to negotiate with the Iranians and can the Iranians and the Americans work out a deal here.

speaker: Guest
text: That's, you know, all beside the point because the fact is the Israelis have no interest in a deal. Remember what happened right after President Trump announced there was a ceasefire.

speaker: Guest
text: The Israelis slammed Lebanon. They attacked Lebanon in a ferocious way. They murdered huge numbers of innocent civilians in Lebanon, all for the purposes of undermining the ceasefire,

speaker: Guest
text: because the ceasefire, as was agreed to by President Trump and the Trump administration more generally, call for Israel to stop attacking Hezbollah. The Iranians insisted on this. Israel cannot attack Hezbollah during the ceasefire.

speaker: Guest
text: And of course, when Trump called up Netanyahu and told him that was one of the principal provisions in the ceasefire, Netanyahu made it clear he did not agree.

speaker: Guest
text: And then he went out and he started bombing Lebanon in large part to undermine the ceasefire because the Israelis don't want a ceasefire. They want the war to go on. They want us to destroy Iran or to produce regime change,

speaker: Guest
text: which has not happened and is not going to happen, which leaves us with one option, destroying Iran to make the Israelis happy.

speaker: Host
text: So what happens at the end of this week when the two weeks of the so-called ceasefire are over?

speaker: Host
text: Does Trump resume bombing or does he await the outcome of his blockade of the blockade? Well, what's going on in his mind? I'm sorry for the triple questions here, Professor. An Easter Sunday in a very profane and most inappropriate tweet.

speaker: Host
text: demanding the Strait of Hormuz be open, now he's blockading it.

speaker: Guest
text: Well, what's going on is that he's desperate. I mean, he understands at this point that he made a colossal blunder starting this war. If we could go back to February 27th, he would not attack Iran knowing what has happened.

speaker: Guest
text: And the question now is, how does he get himself out of this situation? And the problem he faces is that the Iranians are a formidable adversary. They have lots of cards to play. And on the other side, he's dealing with the Israelis,

speaker: Guest
text: who are a formidable adversary, and I'm choosing my words carefully here. The Israelis are a formidable adversary of the United States, and they have very powerful cards to play because they have this institution called the Israel lobby,

speaker: Guest
text: comprised of all sorts of institutions and individuals in the United States who have enormous power and go to great lengths to make sure that U.S. U.S. foreign policy dances to Israel's tune. So Trump is caught between a rock and a hard place.

speaker: Guest
text: He's got the Israelis on one side and the Iranians on the other side, and he has hardly any negotiating room.

speaker: Host
text: What has Netanyahu accomplished by slaughtering civilians in a shopping area in downtown Beirut? Civilians.

speaker: Guest
text: Well, I think the problem that he faces, this is independent of Iran. is that he is doing very poorly against Hezbollah. You want to remember in the years 2023 and 2024,

speaker: Guest
text: it looked like the Israelis had decimated Hezbollah to the point where it was effectively defanged. And then the war against Iran started on February 28th and Hezbollah joined in to help its Iranian allies.

speaker: Guest
text: And it's been quite remarkable how effective Hezbollah is in two different ways, one at lobbying rockets and missiles in large numbers at northern Israel and even into Tel Aviv.

speaker: Guest
text: It's quite clear that Hezbollah in terms of its rocket and missile force has not been defanged. But furthermore, if you look at the battles that have been taking place in southern Lebanon, you want to remember the Israelis are interested.

speaker: Guest
text: interested in conquering southern Lebanon up to the Latani River. The Israelis have really been clobbered by Hezbollah in the, you know, the tank battles and the infantry battles in southern Lebanon.

speaker: Guest
text: And the Israelis have actually pulled back and they're no longer talking about colonizing southern Lebanon up to the Latani River. They now have a very narrow strip that they control along the border between northern Israel.

speaker: Guest
text: Israel and southern Lebanon. So Hezbollah is alive and well. And what I think Netanyahu wants to do is he wants to foment a civil war in Lebanon.

speaker: Guest
text: He wants to foment a war between the government on one side and Hezbollah on the other as a way of weakening Hezbollah and as a way of weakening Lebanon.

speaker: Host
text: That would explain his offer to negotiate, not with Hezbollah, but with the government of Lebanon.

speaker: Guest
text: Absolutely. And there's all sorts of talk in the Israeli press that what the Israelis are interested in doing is getting the Lebanese government to disarm Hezbollah because the Israelis can't disarm Hezbollah, as I was just saying.

speaker: Guest
text: So they want the Lebanese government to do it. But of course, the Lebanese government is in no position.

speaker: Guest
text: to disarm Hezbollah. And many people think that the end result will be a civil war involving Hezbollah on one side and the Lebanese government on the other side. And this will be good for Israel.

speaker: Host
text: Well, I have to change the topic for just a minute. One of the chatters writes in, I won't tell you what the person's email address is. John is my dear grandpa.

speaker: Host
text: So one of your grandchildren is watching us now, Professor Mayor Shommer, and it's a delight. It just excited me to see that.

speaker: Guest
text: I hope my grandchild, whoever he or she is.

speaker: Host
text: I don't want to embarrass the person, but I will give you the email moniker of this person when we're off air. I mean, Trump really has no out anymore, does he?

speaker: Guest
text: He does not have a good out. I mean, he could basically cave in to the Iranian demands, but he would pay an awful price from Israel and from the lobby in the United States. My view on this judge is that. For

speaker: Guest
text: Trump to cave. What has to happen here is that the economy, the international economy, the world economy has to be on the verge of going off the precipice. There has to be enough damage around the world to

speaker: Guest
text: cause President Trump to simply say to the Israelis, enough is enough. We have to shut this down. And this is why I've long argued that from Iran's point of view, the longer this war goes on, the better.

speaker: Guest
text: I think the Iranians were actually foolish to go to Islamabad. If I had been playing their hand, I would not have gone to Islamabad. I would have just kept the war going because they were in the driver's seat. And the longer the war goes on.

speaker: Guest
text: the more leverage they have and I think at some point if the war gets goes on long enough and things get bad enough economically Trump will then have no choice but to clash with the lobby and with Israel and force them to accept some sort of meaningful peace agreement but if we never reach that point this is just going to go on and on

speaker: Host
text: Hmm

speaker: Host
text: Well, the president has a terrible dilemma on his hands. There's no question about that. Do you think he understands?

speaker: Host
text: That's a dumb question on my part. Do you think he understands? Who knows what he understands? Do the people around him know that Netanyahu's goal is to divide Iran the way MI6, CIA, and the Israelis divided Syria?

speaker: Guest
text: I'm sure they do. I mean, they have, we're talking about his advisors here, and I would even imagine him. They understand full well that they are in a desperate situation, that we have to get out of this conflict. You want to remember,

speaker: Guest
text: it was the Americans who pushed for the Islamabad meeting. It was not the Iranians. The Iranians went along with the meeting, but it was the Americans who wanted the meeting.

speaker: Host
text: But the meeting was just performative. I mean, Vance had no authority. It was monitored by the two Zionists. It was reporting to Netanyahu and his demands were outrageous.

speaker: Guest
text: Yes, but what you're missing here is that Trump is caught between two forces. One are the Iranians and the need to shut this down, and two, the Israeli desire to destroy Iran. So what happens is that

speaker: Guest
text: Trump says last Tuesday that we're going to have a ceasefire. This is on April 7th. We're going to have a ceasefire, he says, and he says we're actually going to negotiate on the basis of Iran's 10-point plan.

speaker: Guest
text: And this looks very promising at the moment. But then what happens over the course of the week and what actually happens in the negotiations itself is that the Israelis and the lobby weighs in. And Trump backs off.

speaker: Guest
text: And instead of negotiating with the Iranians in Islamabad on the basis of the 10-point plan that the Iranians had put on the table, he negotiates, contrary to what he said he was going to do,

speaker: Guest
text: on the basis of the 15-point plan that the Americans had put forward earlier, which includes all the maximalist demands that the Israelis want. So what you see is that ultimately we adopt the Israeli position and that's the basis of the negotiations.

speaker: Guest
text: And this is what happens all the time. Anytime we show any flexibility, any interest in accommodating the Iranians, the Israelis and the lobby move in and we adopt the hardline position which reflects Israel's interests.

speaker: Host
text: And as Prime Minister Netanyahu made it clear, we're reporting to him. all along the way. And don't you think that when we report to our boss, Prime Minister Netanyahu,

speaker: Host
text: that he makes it clear to us exactly what he wants and we salute and do what he wants.

speaker: Guest
text: And anytime he senses that we're getting out of line and we're getting too flexible with the Iranians, he calls up President Trump and he tells him in no uncertain terms that he has to get back in line. President Trump salutes, gets back in line.

speaker: Guest
text: And here we are.

speaker: Host
text: I wonder if Netanyahu tricked Trump or threatened Trump.

speaker: Guest
text: It's probably a combination of the two.

speaker: Guest
text: I think that if you look at the New York Times story on what the negotiations or what the discussions looked like between the Israelis and the Americans before the war, before February 28th, it looks like the Israelis bamboozled Trump. They convinced him that they could win this magic.

speaker: Guest
text: magical victory. They sold him a bill. the goods. And he was gullible and he bought it. He didn't listen to his advisors. He didn't ask his advisors tough questions that might have led him to counter the Israeli narrative that was on the table.

speaker: Guest
text: So I think he was bamboozled. But the point that I'm making to you is regardless of what happened in those discussions, the fact is, as we move along here, President Trump fully understands Jamie Vance fully understands,

speaker: Guest
text: Marco Rubio fully understands that they really have little choice but to do what the Israelis and what the lobby wants. As I've long argued,

speaker: Guest
text: Israel is a huge albatross around their neck. And if anybody needs more proof of that than what's happening here, they're a hopeless cause because it's patently obvious from this case. that Israel is causing us huge strategic and moral problems.

speaker: Host
text: Do the Iranians recognize the United States towing the Israeli line?

speaker: Guest
text: Absolutely. I mean, all you have to do is listen to them talk and look at all these memes that they're putting out. Everybody understands this at this point in time, even inside the United States.

speaker: Guest
text: I'm shocked as someone, you know, who along with Steve Wall wrote this book on the Israel lobby in 2007 and an article on the subject in 2006. I'm shocked at how aware most Americans are today.

speaker: Guest
text: of what the lobby and what Israel are doing to influence U.S. policy in a negative way. We've just reached the point, thanks to the alternative media,

speaker: Guest
text: that lots and lots of people in the United States fully understand what's happening. And you go around the world, especially to places like Iran, and they understand full well.

speaker: Host
text: Well, Professor Merchanra, thank you very much, my dear friend. If you stay with us for a moment, I'll give you the name of... Or the name used by the person who said you are his or her dear grandpa. But thank you, as always, for your analysis.

speaker: Host
text: And we'll look forward to seeing you next week.

speaker: Guest
text: My pleasure as always, Judge, and I look forward to seeing you next week.

speaker: Host
text: Thank you. Coming up at 10 o'clock this morning, Aaron Mate at two this afternoon, Matt Ho at three this afternoon, Colonel Karen Kratkowski, Judge Napolitano for Judging Freedom.
